# Handoff — rule-overlap dedup follow-up after review-close

## Shorthand And Placeholder Legend
- `FOLLOWUP` = ticket [66df8683-1e3e-44d5-9052-4bb72277dc57](./.ticket/tickets/66df8683-1e3e-44d5-9052-4bb72277dc57/ticket.toml), title: `[rule-audit][prompts] Deduplicate high-overlap rules from fresh overlap audit`
- `REVIEW` = ticket [474eb962-b68f-4651-b980-c4c9233b2710](./.ticket/tickets/474eb962-b68f-4651-b980-c4c9233b2710/ticket.toml), title: `[session-api][audit-cli] Plan policy-file updates for session_audit and schema-version guidance`
- `WORKTREE-NOISE` = unrelated open ticket edits in [./.ticket/tickets/db9bad13-ae43-4300-8037-7165c0e9a7b0/description.md](./.ticket/tickets/db9bad13-ae43-4300-8037-7165c0e9a7b0/description.md) and [./.ticket/tickets/db9bad13-ae43-4300-8037-7165c0e9a7b0/history.ndjson](./.ticket/tickets/db9bad13-ae43-4300-8037-7165c0e9a7b0/history.ndjson)

## Ticket Legend
- `FOLLOWUP` -> [66df8683-1e3e-44d5-9052-4bb72277dc57](./.ticket/tickets/66df8683-1e3e-44d5-9052-4bb72277dc57/ticket.toml) — `[rule-audit][prompts] Deduplicate high-overlap rules from fresh overlap audit`
- `REVIEW` -> [474eb962-b68f-4651-b980-c4c9233b2710](./.ticket/tickets/474eb962-b68f-4651-b980-c4c9233b2710/ticket.toml) — `[session-api][audit-cli] Plan policy-file updates for session_audit and schema-version guidance`

## Overview
The review-hardening track is complete. The overlap-audit checkpoint was verified, the mistaken “metric absent” reading was corrected, a dedicated follow-up ticket for the remaining dedup work was created and committed, and the remaining work is now a clean content-dedup slice rather than an audit-ergonomics or prompt-regeneration problem. The handoff should start from `FOLLOWUP`, not from `REVIEW`.

## Findings
1. The structured overlap metric is present and usable in `audit run` output at `metrics.rule_overlap`; the earlier `null` result came from reading a non-existent top-level `rule_overlap` field.
2. Fresh audit evidence from `rtk cargo run -p audit-cli --bin audit -- --json run .` showed:
- `status=collected`
- `rules_considered=298`
- `compared_pairs=44253`
- `high_overlap_pairs=53`
- `max_similarity=1.0`
3. The same audit run emitted concrete `rule_overlap` findings, including 100% lexical-overlap pairs across recurring-principle and agent/prompt-adjacent entries.
4. `rule sync-targets --config rule-targets.yaml` was rerun after the review update and completed with `status=ok`; the reviewed policy surfaces stayed drift-free.
5. The remaining work is now explicitly tracked by `FOLLOWUP`, created with the audit evidence and acceptance criteria needed for the next session.
6. The ticket-store update for this handoff was committed as Git commit `1c89221c` with message `chore(tickets): track rule-overlap dedup follow-up`.

## Decisions
1. Do not create a separate “metric absent” or audit-output ergonomics ticket; that branch was disproven by the corrected structured selector.
2. Treat the remaining work as rule-content deduplication with canonical-owner cleanup, not prompt regeneration or handoff-policy repair.
3. Keep the next session path-scoped to the rule and generated prompt/instruction surfaces implicated by the overlap findings.
4. Ignore `WORKTREE-NOISE`; those `db9bad13` ticket edits are unrelated to this overlap-cleanup track and were intentionally left unstaged.

## Blockers And Risks
1. No hard blocker exists for `FOLLOWUP`; the board is clear and the follow-up ticket is in `new`.
2. The main risk is false-positive cleanup from broad grep or summary reading. Use the exact structured `rule_overlap` findings as the source of truth for which rule ids/slugs to touch.
3. The workspace still contains unrelated modified files under `WORKTREE-NOISE`; do not mix them into this follow-up unless you intentionally switch tracks.

## Long-Horizon Goal
Keep new-session guidance deterministic and review-ready by reducing duplicated rule content, preserving one canonical owner per repeated instruction, and validating the cleanup with fresh overlap-audit evidence after each batch.

## Key Entities
1. [./.ticket/tickets/66df8683-1e3e-44d5-9052-4bb72277dc57/ticket.toml](./.ticket/tickets/66df8683-1e3e-44d5-9052-4bb72277dc57/ticket.toml)
Why it matters: this is the new authoritative ticket for the remaining overlap-dedup work.
2. [./.ticket/tickets/66df8683-1e3e-44d5-9052-4bb72277dc57/description.md](./.ticket/tickets/66df8683-1e3e-44d5-9052-4bb72277dc57/description.md)
Why it matters: contains the scope, acceptance criteria, and evidence anchors seeded from the audit run.
3. [./.ticket/tickets/474eb962-b68f-4651-b980-c4c9233b2710/description.md](./.ticket/tickets/474eb962-b68f-4651-b980-c4c9233b2710/description.md)
Why it matters: captures the corrected review-close evidence, including the overlap metric and the successful regeneration pass.
4. [./.agents/instructions/audit.instructions.md](./.agents/instructions/audit.instructions.md)
Why it matters: already contains the rule-audit manual expectations; this surface is no longer the remaining problem.
5. [./.agents/prompts/handoff.prompt.md](./.agents/prompts/handoff.prompt.md)
Why it matters: generated handoff prompt stayed drift-free after regeneration; do not re-open it unless a specific overlap finding points back here.
6. [./.agents/prompts/handoff-tickets.prompt.md](./.agents/prompts/handoff-tickets.prompt.md)
Why it matters: companion generated handoff surface that also stayed drift-free.
7. [./.rule/rules/084fd4e6-660b-4227-a13e-514edf44e393/body.md](./.rule/rules/084fd4e6-660b-4227-a13e-514edf44e393/body.md)
Why it matters: canonical handoff rule source, useful if one of the high-overlap pairs includes handoff-specific wording.
8. [./.rule/rules/a634822a-0f7d-407f-b313-b6465b647d2f/body.md](./.rule/rules/a634822a-0f7d-407f-b313-b6465b647d2f/body.md)
Why it matters: canonical handoff-tickets rule source, another likely overlap candidate.
9. [./.rule/rules/a7c0f852-b754-436f-a09c-36395594fd50/body.md](./.rule/rules/a7c0f852-b754-436f-a09c-36395594fd50/body.md)
Why it matters: canonical audit-instruction rule source; useful reference for keeping overlap-audit guidance centralized.
10. [./memory-api/tools/cli/audit-cli/README.md](./memory-api/tools/cli/audit-cli/README.md)
Why it matters: documents the audit surface the next session should use for repeated overlap checks.
11. [./memory-api/tools/cli/audit-cli/src/cli.rs](./memory-api/tools/cli/audit-cli/src/cli.rs)
Why it matters: authoritative implementation of the structured/human overlap metric surface that was just validated.

## Next Actions
1. Start from `FOLLOWUP`: open [./.ticket/tickets/66df8683-1e3e-44d5-9052-4bb72277dc57/ticket.toml](./.ticket/tickets/66df8683-1e3e-44d5-9052-4bb72277dc57/ticket.toml) and move it into `in-implementation` when you begin the cleanup.
2. Re-run the overlap audit in structured form:
- `rtk cargo run -p audit-cli --bin audit -- --json run .`
- read `metrics.rule_overlap`
- extract only `findings[]` where `category == "rule_overlap"`
3. Pick one small dedup batch first, preferably one of the 100% overlap pairs affecting:
- handoff-adjacent prompt/rule content, or
- recurring-principle duplicates spanning repos/scopes
4. For that batch, choose one canonical owner rule, trim duplicated wording from the secondary rules, and keep only thin references where duplication is intentional.
5. After each edit batch, run:
- `rule sync-targets --config rule-targets.yaml`
- `rtk cargo run -p audit-cli --bin audit -- --json run .`
6. Record before/after overlap evidence on `FOLLOWUP`, including whether `high_overlap_pairs` decreased and which duplicates were intentionally retained.
7. Keep the commit scope restricted to the overlap-cleanup ticket and the rule/generated files touched by the selected batch.
8. Continue until at least one concrete overlap batch is deduplicated or the work becomes explicitly blocked with evidence.

## First Concrete Action
Run the structured audit again and isolate the top `rule_overlap` findings before choosing any file to edit.

## First Validation Check
Confirm the rerun still reports a valid `metrics.rule_overlap` object and that the selected overlap pair is present in `findings[]` with `category == "rule_overlap"`.

## Completion Status
1. Create follow-up ticket: completed
Note: `FOLLOWUP` was created and committed.
2. Commit review-close evidence and follow-up tracking: completed
Note: committed in `1c89221c`.
3. Verify audit metric presence and regeneration cleanliness: completed
Note: overlap metric and drift-free regeneration are recorded on `REVIEW`.
4. Deduplicate all concrete overlap batches: not-started
Note: this is the first remaining implementation step.
5. Re-run overlap audit after cleanup: not-started
Note: required after each dedup batch.
6. Record before/after evidence on `FOLLOWUP`: not-started
Note: acceptance evidence still needs to be written by the next session.

## Definition Of Done
1. `FOLLOWUP` contains at least one completed dedup batch based on concrete `rule_overlap` findings.
2. One canonical owner is established per cleaned overlap pair, with secondary rules trimmed or reduced to thin references.
3. `rule sync-targets --config rule-targets.yaml` remains deterministic after the cleanup.
4. A fresh overlap audit is recorded on `FOLLOWUP` with before/after evidence, including whether `high_overlap_pairs` decreased.
5. No unrelated ticket-store or implementation-track changes are mixed into the cleanup commit.

## Board And Persisted-Session Note
1. Board state at handoff time is clear: `active=0`, `stale=0`, `conflict=0`.
2. There is no active board entry to resume; check in on `FOLLOWUP` when implementation starts.
3. Persisted session artifacts are not needed for this restart path because the durable findings are already captured in `REVIEW` and `FOLLOWUP`.
4. `WORKTREE-NOISE` remains in the repo and should be ignored unless you intentionally switch to the `db9bad13` track.

Execute the next actions in order and continue until all are completed or explicitly blocked.

data_json.content: One validation command tripped over shell quoting and made `jq` look like it was written by a sleep-deprived intern. The important part already passed: the targeted pair query returned `[]`. I’m rerunning the post-check in a less fragile form and checking the actual submodule diffs instead of pretending root `git status` can see inside nested repos by telepathy.

data_json.content: The remaining overlaps are clustered, not random: repeated `roast`, `ticket-refinement`, and a smaller set of shared-agent-rule duplicates. I’m reading the owning rule bodies for one agent family and one shared-rule family so I can tell whether these are intentional mirrors or just the same text rubber-stamped across repos.

data.content: The remaining overlaps are clustered, not random: repeated `roast`, `ticket-refinement`, and a smaller set of shared-agent-rule duplicates. I’m reading the owning rule bodies for one agent family and one shared-rule family so I can tell whether these are intentional mirrors or just the same text rubber-stamped across repos.

data_json.content: The `roast` and `ticket-refinement` overlaps look like repo-specific copies of the same agent charter, which is usually a sign that one source should exist and the rest should just point at it. I’m reading the exact rule bodies now to see whether the local variants carry any real repo-specific meaning or just changed the letterhead.

data.content: The `roast` and `ticket-refinement` overlaps look like repo-specific copies of the same agent charter, which is usually a sign that one source should exist and the rest should just point at it. I’m reading the exact rule bodies now to see whether the local variants carry any real repo-specific meaning or just changed the letterhead.

data_json.content: These are literal clones, not nuanced local variants. I’m locating the agent source files that generate them so I can collapse whole repeated sections per repo instead of hand-editing three isolated rule entries and discovering the next paragraph copied itself too.

data.content: These are literal clones, not nuanced local variants. I’m locating the agent source files that generate them so I can collapse whole repeated sections per repo instead of hand-editing three isolated rule entries and discovering the next paragraph copied itself too.

data_json.content: The copies aren’t living as standalone markdown files under those subtrees, which suggests they’re being pulled in through rule-target wiring rather than maintained by humans with too much free time. I’m tracing the rule-target definitions for the agent entries so I can change the actual source of repetition instead of decorating the symptom.

data.content: The copies aren’t living as standalone markdown files under those subtrees, which suggests they’re being pulled in through rule-target wiring rather than maintained by humans with too much free time. I’m tracing the rule-target definitions for the agent entries so I can change the actual source of repetition instead of decorating the symptom.

data_json.content: The overlap source is clearer now: each repo has its own `.github/agents/*.agent.md` copy, and the rule store is just reflecting that duplication. I’m reading those source agent files directly, because that’s the lever that can collapse whole overlap families in one go.

data.content: The overlap source is clearer now: each repo has its own `.github/agents/*.agent.md` copy, and the rule store is just reflecting that duplication. I’m reading those source agent files directly, because that’s the lever that can collapse whole overlap families in one go.

data_json.content: Those paths are recorded as rule scopes, not actual files in this checkout, which is annoyingly on-brand for generated metadata. I’m reading the rule metadata and the submodule directory layout next so I can find the real source files instead of chasing phantom paths.

data.content: Those paths are recorded as rule scopes, not actual files in this checkout, which is annoyingly on-brand for generated metadata. I’m reading the rule metadata and the submodule directory layout next so I can find the real source files instead of chasing phantom paths.

data_json.content: The submodule roots do have their own agent sources; the earlier miss was just me asking the filesystem the wrong question in the right tone. I’m reading the actual agent source files now so I can decide whether to centralize them or make the local copies intentionally divergent and slim.

data.content: The submodule roots do have their own agent sources; the earlier miss was just me asking the filesystem the wrong question in the right tone. I’m reading the actual agent source files now so I can decide whether to centralize them or make the local copies intentionally divergent and slim.

data_json.content: The submodule roots have `.github`, but not necessarily the `agents` folder the rule metadata remembers. I’m listing those `.github` directories directly to see whether these overlaps are pointing at deleted historical sources, which would change the right fix.

data.content: The submodule roots have `.github`, but not necessarily the `agents` folder the rule metadata remembers. I’m listing those `.github` directories directly to see whether these overlaps are pointing at deleted historical sources, which would change the right fix.

-## Quality Gates
-
-- Relevant validation must pass before completion. If a required check repeatedly fails, stop expanding scope and record the failing command, log or manual result, and blocker clearly in the ticket/spec status summary.
-- Before a ticket moves to `in-review`, ensure the relevant spec is updated for the changed requirements or goals and links the related tickets, updated docs, and test or validation results.
-- **Browser verification is mandatory** for any change to a server interface or frontend feature:
-  open the affected viewer in an external fullscreen Chromium-family browser, not VS Code's integrated browser, and confirm the feature works visually before marking work done.
-- Record the browser window or display resolution used for manual visual validation whenever layout, rendering, or responsive behavior could affect the result.
-- **Write Playwright end-to-end tests** for all browser-facing features and server interface changes.
-  When executing browser-hosted frontend checks, first try the MCP Playwright/browser tools. Fall back to repo-local Playwright commands only when the MCP surface is unavailable or cannot cover the scenario.
-  Shared managed-viewer suites live under `viewer-api/viewer-api/frontend/dioxus/e2e/shared/`.
-  Spec-viewer release E2E lives under `memory-viewers/spec-viewer/frontend/dioxus/`; run it with `npm run test:e2e:release`.
-  Ticket-viewer release E2E lives under `memory-viewers/ticket-viewer/frontend/dioxus/`; run it with `npm run test:e2e:release`.
-  Doc-viewer and log-viewer keep local Playwright wrappers under `tools/viewer/doc-viewer/e2e/` and `tools/viewer/log-viewer/e2e/`, importing shared suites from `memory-viewers/viewer-api`.
-- For tracing-based tests, use:

-## Escalation Rules
-
-- If blocked by ambiguity after focused research (10-15 minutes), ask the user.
-- If evidence conflicts or architecture tradeoffs are required, ask before committing to a direction.
-- In multi-agent workspaces, treat unrelated workspace changes as expected background activity and continue.
-- Before editing, claim ownership of the files you will touch; commit only your owned changes, and release ownership when done.
-- Ignore unrelated changes by default; do not interrupt work solely because they exist.
-- Escalate only when unrelated changes create a real conflict with your owned scope (for example merge conflicts, overlapping owned paths, or failures directly caused by those changes).
-- Never revert, stage, or commit unrelated changes created by other agents.

-## Feedback Workflow
-
-- Record feedback on canonical rule entries today. `spec-api` entries do not yet expose direct feedback tools or feedback summary fields.
-- When feedback came from a specific generated spec or instruction surface, first locate the canonical rule entry that produced that text. Use `rule search` or `rule_list` / `rule_search` with `repo_scope`, `path_scope`, `section`, or `slug` filters until you have the exact rule ID or slug.
-- Record the feedback on that rule entry with either:
-  - CLI: `rule feedback <id-or-slug> --rating helpful|mixed|not-helpful [--note "..."] [--note-kind note|suggestion] [--session-id <id> --agent-or-user-id <id>]`
-  - MCP: `rule_record_feedback` with `id`, `rating`, optional `note`, optional `note_kind`, and the `session_id` + `agent_or_user_id` pair when a manual session reference is needed.
-- If you are reacting to a native spec entry rather than generated rule text, include the spec ID, path, and section in the feedback note text and open or update the corresponding spec or ticket work. Do not describe that as direct spec-entry feedback when the current storage is rule-entry scoped.
-- Review follow-up queues with `rule list --low-rated-only`, `rule list --unresolved-only`, or the MCP `rule_list` / `rule_search` flags `low_rated_only` and `unresolved_only`.

-## Feedback Workflow
-
-- Record feedback on canonical rule entries today. `spec-api` entries do not yet expose direct feedback tools or feedback summary fields.

-- Record the feedback on that rule entry with either:
-  - CLI: `rule feedback <id-or-slug> --rating helpful|mixed|not-helpful [--note "..."] [--note-kind note|suggestion] [--session-id <id> --agent-or-user-id <id>]`
-  - MCP: `rule_record_feedback` with `id`, `rating`, optional `note`, optional `note_kind`, and the `session_id` + `agent_or_user_id` pair when a manual session reference is needed.
-- If you are reacting to a native spec entry rather than generated rule text, include the spec ID, path, and section in the feedback note text and open or update the corresponding spec or ticket work. Do not describe that as direct spec-entry feedback when the current storage is rule-entry scoped.
-- Review follow-up queues with `rule list --low-rated-only`, `rule list --unresolved-only`, or the MCP `rule_list` / `rule_search` flags `low_rated_only` and `unresolved_only`.

-## Task Routing
-

-## Escalation Rules
-
-- If blocked by ambiguity after focused research (10-15 minutes), ask the user.
-- If evidence conflicts or architecture tradeoffs are required, ask before committing to a direction.
-- In multi-agent workspaces, treat unrelated workspace changes as expected background activity and continue.
-- Before editing, claim ownership of the files you will touch; commit only your owned changes, and release ownership when done.
-- Ignore unrelated changes by default; do not interrupt work solely because they exist.
-- Escalate only when unrelated changes create a real conflict with your owned scope (for example merge conflicts, overlapping owned paths, or failures directly caused by those changes).
-- Never revert, stage, or commit unrelated changes created by other agents.

-## Feedback Workflow
-
-- Record feedback on canonical rule entries today. `spec-api` entries do not yet expose direct feedback tools or feedback summary fields.
-- When feedback came from a specific generated spec or instruction surface, first locate the canonical rule entry that produced that text. Use `rule search` or `rule_list` / `rule_search` with `repo_scope`, `path_scope`, `section`, or `slug` filters until you have the exact rule ID or slug.
-- Record the feedback on that rule entry with either:
-  - CLI: `rule feedback <id-or-slug> --rating helpful|mixed|not-helpful [--note "..."] [--note-kind note|suggestion] [--session-id <id> --agent-or-user-id <id>]`
-  - MCP: `rule_record_feedback` with `id`, `rating`, optional `note`, optional `note_kind`, and the `session_id` + `agent_or_user_id` pair when a manual session reference is needed.
-- If you are reacting to a native spec entry rather than generated rule text, include the spec ID, path, and section in the feedback note text and open or update the corresponding spec or ticket work. Do not describe that as direct spec-entry feedback when the current storage is rule-entry scoped.
-- Review follow-up queues with `rule list --low-rated-only`, `rule list --unresolved-only`, or the MCP `rule_list` / `rule_search` flags `low_rated_only` and `unresolved_only`.

-## Feedback Workflow
-
-- Record feedback on canonical rule entries today. `spec-api` entries do not yet expose direct feedback tools or feedback summary fields.
-- When feedback came from a specific generated spec or instruction surface, first locate the canonical rule entry that produced that text. Use `rule search` or `rule_list` / `rule_search` with `repo_scope`, `path_scope`, `section`, or `slug` filters until you have the exact rule ID or slug.
-- Record the feedback on that rule entry with either:
-  - CLI: `rule feedback <id-or-slug> --rating helpful|mixed|not-helpful [--note "..."] [--note-kind note|suggestion] [--session-id <id> --agent-or-user-id <id>]`
-  - MCP: `rule_record_feedback` with `id`, `rating`, optional `note`, optional `note_kind`, and the `session_id` + `agent_or_user_id` pair when a manual session reference is needed.
-- If you are reacting to a native spec entry rather than generated rule text, include the spec ID, path, and section in the feedback note text and open or update the corresponding spec or ticket work. Do not describe that as direct spec-entry feedback when the current storage is rule-entry scoped.
-- Review follow-up queues with `rule list --low-rated-only`, `rule list --unresolved-only`, or the MCP `rule_list` / `rule_search` flags `low_rated_only` and `unresolved_only`.

-## Task Routing
-
-- Any requested implementation or behavior change: create or update the tracking ticket(s) first, then create or update the relevant spec before editing files.
-- Simple fix (1-2 files): after the ticket/spec setup when requirements or behavior change, gather context, implement, validate, update docs, verify spec links, and move the ticket to `in-review`.
-- Bug fix: after the ticket/spec setup, follow `.agents/prompts/debug-test.prompt.md` when available.
-- Feature or refactor (>5 files, >100 LOC, or unclear scope): use `.agents/prompts/tickets.prompt.md` to establish the ticket set, then `.agents/prompts/spec.prompt.md` to update the spec before implementation.
-- Unfamiliar module or unclear behavior: follow `.agents/prompts/research.prompt.md` when available before locking the spec or implementation plan.
-- Swarm execution: use `.agents/prompts/swarm-worker.prompt.md`.

-## Quality Gates
-
-- Relevant validation must pass before completion. If a required check repeatedly fails, stop expanding scope and record the failing command, log or manual result, and blocker clearly in the ticket/spec status summary.
-- Before a ticket moves to `in-review`, ensure the relevant spec is updated for the changed requirements or goals and links the related tickets, updated docs, and test or validation results.
-- **Browser verification is mandatory** for any change to a server interface or frontend feature:
-  open the affected viewer in an external fullscreen Chromium-family browser, not VS Code's integrated browser, and confirm the feature works visually before marking work done.
-- Record the browser window or display resolution used for manual visual validation whenever layout, rendering, or responsive behavior could affect the result.
-- **Write Playwright end-to-end tests** for all browser-facing features and server interface changes.
-  When executing browser-hosted frontend checks, first try the MCP Playwright/browser tools. Fall back to repo-local Playwright commands only when the MCP surface is unavailable or cannot cover the scenario.
-  Shared managed-viewer suites live under `viewer-api/viewer-api/frontend/dioxus/e2e/shared/`.
-  Spec-viewer release E2E lives under `memory-viewers/spec-viewer/frontend/dioxus/`; run it with `npm run test:e2e:release`.
-  Ticket-viewer release E2E lives under `memory-viewers/ticket-viewer/frontend/dioxus/`; run it with `npm run test:e2e:release`.
-  Doc-viewer and log-viewer keep local Playwright wrappers under `tools/viewer/doc-viewer/e2e/` and `tools/viewer/log-viewer/e2e/`, importing shared suites from `memory-viewers/viewer-api`.
-- For tracing-based tests, use:

-## Escalation Rules
-

-- If evidence conflicts or architecture tradeoffs are required, ask before committing to a direction.
-- In multi-agent workspaces, treat unrelated workspace changes as expected background activity and continue.
-- Before editing, claim ownership of the files you will touch; commit only your owned changes, and release ownership when done.
-- Ignore unrelated changes by default; do not interrupt work solely because they exist.
-- Escalate only when unrelated changes create a real conflict with your owned scope (for example merge conflicts, overlapping owned paths, or failures directly caused by those changes).
-- Never revert, stage, or commit unrelated changes created by other agents.

-## Feedback Workflow
-
-- Record feedback on canonical rule entries today. `spec-api` entries do not yet expose direct feedback tools or feedback summary fields.
-- When feedback came from a specific generated spec or instruction surface, first locate the canonical rule entry that produced that text. Use `rule search` or `rule_list` / `rule_search` with `repo_scope`, `path_scope`, `section`, or `slug` filters until you have the exact rule ID or slug.
-- Record the feedback on that rule entry with either:
-  - CLI: `rule feedback <id-or-slug> --rating helpful|mixed|not-helpful [--note "..."] [--note-kind note|suggestion] [--session-id <id> --agent-or-user-id <id>]`
-  - MCP: `rule_record_feedback` with `id`, `rating`, optional `note`, optional `note_kind`, and the `session_id` + `agent_or_user_id` pair when a manual session reference is needed.
-- If you are reacting to a native spec entry rather than generated rule text, include the spec ID, path, and section in the feedback note text and open or update the corresponding spec or ticket work. Do not describe that as direct spec-entry feedback when the current storage is rule-entry scoped.
-- Review follow-up queues with `rule list --low-rated-only`, `rule list --unresolved-only`, or the MCP `rule_list` / `rule_search` flags `low_rated_only` and `unresolved_only`.

-## Task Routing
-
-- Any requested implementation or behavior change: create or update the tracking ticket(s) first, then create or update the relevant spec before editing files.
-- Simple fix (1-2 files): after the ticket/spec setup when requirements or behavior change, gather context, implement, validate, update docs, verify spec links, and move the ticket to `in-review`.
-- Bug fix: after the ticket/spec setup, follow `.agents/prompts/debug-test.prompt.md` when available.
-- Feature or refactor (>5 files, >100 LOC, or unclear scope): use `.agents/prompts/tickets.prompt.md` to establish the ticket set, then `.agents/prompts/spec.prompt.md` to update the spec before implementation.
-- Unfamiliar module or unclear behavior: follow `.agents/prompts/research.prompt.md` when available before locking the spec or implementation plan.
-- Swarm execution: use `.agents/prompts/swarm-worker.prompt.md`.

-## Quality Gates
-
-- Relevant validation must pass before completion. If a required check repeatedly fails, stop expanding scope and record the failing command, log or manual result, and blocker clearly in the ticket/spec status summary.
-- Before a ticket moves to `in-review`, ensure the relevant spec is updated for the changed requirements or goals and links the related tickets, updated docs, and test or validation results.
-- **Browser verification is mandatory** for any change to a server interface or frontend feature:
-  open the affected viewer in an external fullscreen Chromium-family browser, not VS Code's integrated browser, and confirm the feature works visually before marking work done.
-- Record the browser window or display resolution used for manual visual validation whenever layout, rendering, or responsive behavior could affect the result.
-- **Write Playwright end-to-end tests** for all browser-facing features and server interface changes.
-  When executing browser-hosted frontend checks, first try the MCP Playwright/browser tools. Fall back to repo-local Playwright commands only when the MCP surface is unavailable or cannot cover the scenario.
-  Shared managed-viewer suites live under `viewer-api/viewer-api/frontend/dioxus/e2e/shared/`.
-  Spec-viewer release E2E lives under `memory-viewers/spec-viewer/frontend/dioxus/`; run it with `npm run test:e2e:release`.
-  Ticket-viewer release E2E lives under `memory-viewers/ticket-viewer/frontend/dioxus/`; run it with `npm run test:e2e:release`.
-  Doc-viewer and log-viewer keep local Playwright wrappers under `tools/viewer/doc-viewer/e2e/` and `tools/viewer/log-viewer/e2e/`, importing shared suites from `memory-viewers/viewer-api`.
-- For tracing-based tests, use:

-## Escalation Rules
-
-- If blocked by ambiguity after focused research (10-15 minutes), ask the user.
-- If evidence conflicts or architecture tradeoffs are required, ask before committing to a direction.
-- In multi-agent workspaces, treat unrelated workspace changes as expected background activity and continue.
-- Before editing, claim ownership of the files you will touch; commit only your owned changes, and release ownership when done.
-- Ignore unrelated changes by default; do not interrupt work solely because they exist.
-- Escalate only when unrelated changes create a real conflict with your owned scope (for example merge conflicts, overlapping owned paths, or failures directly caused by those changes).
-- Never revert, stage, or commit unrelated changes created by other agents.

 # Implement
 
-Make the smallest correct change that satisfies the requested behavior, validate it immediately, and return a concise evidence-backed summary.
-
-Reference [AGENTS.md](./AGENTS.md) and [commit.instructions.md](./.agents/instructions/commit.instructions.md).
-
-## Scope
-
-- Implement narrow fixes and bounded features once the target slice is clear.
-- Work from the owning code path, nearby tests, and existing guidance instead of broad repo tours.
-- Keep edits small, local, and reversible until the first focused validation passes.
-- Update nearby tests, docs, specs, and ticket evidence only when the changed behavior requires it.
-
-## Constraints
-
-- Prefer surgical edits over broad refactors.
-- Do not spend tokens on long research or narration once you have the owning slice.
-- Before the first edit, gather only enough context to state one falsifiable local hypothesis and one cheap disconfirming check.
-- After the first substantive edit, run the narrowest focused validation before more reading or patching.
-- If the first validation fails, repair the same slice or take one nearby hop to the controlling code path; do not reopen broad exploration.
-- Keep status output brief and implementation-focused.
-- Stop and ask for direction only when a focused search still leaves a material product or architecture ambiguity.
-
-## Required Workflow
-
-1. Anchor on a concrete ticket, failing behavior, file, symbol, or generated target.
-2. Check the nearest owning code path, related ticket/spec context, and one neighboring test or call site.
-3. State one local hypothesis and the first cheap falsifying check.
-4. Make the smallest grounded edit that tests or implements that hypothesis.
-5. Run the first focused validation immediately after that edit.
-6. Iterate locally until the slice is correct, then summarize the result and evidence with minimal extra narration.
-
-## Response
-
-Return:
-- implementation target and owning slice
-- hypothesis and first check
-- edits made
-- validation run
-- remaining risk, if any
-- next action or done
+Delegate to the Implement Agent for the full surgical workflow.
+
+Use this prompt as a thin wrapper: provide the concrete target, rely on the agent contract for the detailed implementation loop, and return only the evidence-backed summary needed for the user.
+
+Reference [AGENTS.md](./AGENTS.md) and [commit.instructions.md](./.agents/instructions/commit.instructions.md) when the implementing agent needs repository-specific guardrails.
*** End Patch
